{"id": "tblRelocations01", "name": "Relocations", "views": "[{\"id\": \"viwAllRelocs01\", \"name\": \"All Relocations\", \"type\": \"grid\"}, {\"id\": \"viwByStatus001\", \"name\": \"By Status\", \"type\": \"kanban\"}]", "fields": "[{\"id\": \"fldRelocName001\", \"name\": \"Name\", \"type\": \"singleLineText\", \"description\": \"Full name of the person relocating\"}, {\"id\": \"fldRelocComp01\", \"name\": \"Company\", \"type\": \"singleLineText\", \"description\": \"Client company requesting the relocation\"}, {\"id\": \"fldRelocStat01\", \"name\": \"Status\", \"type\": \"singleSelect\", \"description\": \"Current status of the relocation\", \"options\": {\"choices\": [{\"name\": \"Inquiry\", \"color\": \"gray\"}, {\"name\": \"In Progress\", \"color\": \"blue\"}, {\"name\": \"Completed\", \"color\": \"green\"}, {\"name\": \"Cancelled\", \"color\": \"red\"}]}}, {\"id\": \"fldRelocOrig01\", \"name\": \"Origin\", \"type\": \"singleLineText\", \"description\": \"City the person is relocating from\"}, {\"id\": \"fldRelocDest01\", \"name\": \"Destination\", \"type\": \"singleLineText\", \"description\": \"City the person is relocating to\"}, {\"id\": \"fldRelocStart1\", \"name\": \"Move Start Date\", \"type\": \"date\", \"description\": \"Date movers begin packing/pickup\"}, {\"id\": \"fldRelocEnd001\", \"name\": \"Move End Date\", \"type\": \"date\", \"description\": \"Date of delivery at destination\"}, {\"id\": \"fldRelocCoord1\", \"name\": \"Assigned Coordinator\", \"type\": \"singleLineText\", \"description\": \"MoveOps relocation coordinator handling this move\"}, {\"id\": \"fldRelocReqs01\", \"name\": \"Special Requirements\", \"type\": \"multilineText\", \"description\": \"Any special handling, pet needs, or other requirements\"}]", "base_id": "appMoveOpsOps001", "description": "Tracks all employee relocations managed by MoveOps"}

{"id": "tblStipends00001", "name": "Stipend Transactions", "views": "[{\"id\": \"viwAllStip0001\", \"name\": \"All Transactions\", \"type\": \"grid\"}, {\"id\": \"viwPending0001\", \"name\": \"Pending Review\", \"type\": \"grid\"}]", "fields": "[{\"id\": \"fldStipEmpl001\", \"name\": \"Employee Name\", \"type\": \"singleLineText\", \"description\": \"Name of the employee submitting the expense\"}, {\"id\": \"fldStipAcct001\", \"name\": \"Account\", \"type\": \"singleLineText\", \"description\": \"Client company account\"}, {\"id\": \"fldStipCat0001\", \"name\": \"Expense Category\", \"type\": \"singleLineText\", \"description\": \"Category of the expense\"}, {\"id\": \"fldStipAmt0001\", \"name\": \"Amount Requested\", \"type\": \"number\", \"description\": \"Dollar amount requested by the employee\"}, {\"id\": \"fldStipRcpt001\", \"name\": \"Has Receipt\", \"type\": \"checkbox\", \"description\": \"Whether a receipt was attached to the submission\"}, {\"id\": \"fldStipDate001\", \"name\": \"Date\", \"type\": \"date\", \"description\": \"Date the expense was submitted\"}, {\"id\": \"fldStipAppr001\", \"name\": \"Approval Status\", \"type\": \"singleSelect\", \"description\": \"Review status of the expense\", \"options\": {\"choices\": [{\"name\": \"Pending\", \"color\": \"yellow\"}, {\"name\": \"Approved\", \"color\": \"green\"}, {\"name\": \"Partially Approved\", \"color\": \"blue\"}, {\"name\": \"Rejected\", \"color\": \"red\"}]}}, {\"id\": \"fldStipApAm001\", \"name\": \"Approved Amount\", \"type\": \"number\", \"description\": \"Dollar amount approved (may differ from requested)\"}, {\"id\": \"fldStipNote001\", \"name\": \"Review Notes\", \"type\": \"multilineText\", \"description\": \"Reviewer notes \\u2014 rejection reason, partial approval explanation, or other comments\"}]", "base_id": "appMoveOpsOps001", "description": "Tracks home-office stipend expense submissions and approvals for client employees"}

{"id": "tblClientAccts01", "name": "Client Accounts", "views": "[{\"id\": \"viwAllAccts001\", \"name\": \"All Clients\", \"type\": \"grid\"}, {\"id\": \"viwProspects01\", \"name\": \"Prospects\", \"type\": \"grid\"}]", "fields": "[{\"id\": \"fldAcctClient1\", \"name\": \"Client\", \"type\": \"singleLineText\", \"description\": \"Client company name\"}, {\"id\": \"fldAcctMgr0001\", \"name\": \"Account Manager\", \"type\": \"singleLineText\", \"description\": \"MoveOps account manager owning the relationship\"}, {\"id\": \"fldAcctStart01\", \"name\": \"Relationship Start Date\", \"type\": \"date\", \"description\": \"When the client was first onboarded\"}, {\"id\": \"fldAcctContr01\", \"name\": \"Contract Type\", \"type\": \"singleLineText\", \"description\": \"Service package the client is subscribed to\"}, {\"id\": \"fldAcctStip001\", \"name\": \"Monthly Stipend Fee\", \"type\": \"number\", \"description\": \"Monthly SaaS fee for stipend platform (null if not subscribed)\"}, {\"id\": \"fldAcctReloc01\", \"name\": \"Total Relocations\", \"type\": \"number\", \"description\": \"Total number of relocations completed or in progress for this client\"}, {\"id\": \"fldAcctStat001\", \"name\": \"Status\", \"type\": \"singleSelect\", \"description\": \"Current status of the client relationship\", \"options\": {\"choices\": [{\"name\": \"Active\", \"color\": \"green\"}, {\"name\": \"Onboarding\", \"color\": \"blue\"}, {\"name\": \"Churned\", \"color\": \"red\"}, {\"name\": \"Prospect\", \"color\": \"orange\"}, {\"name\": \"Cold Outreach\", \"color\": \"gray\"}]}}, {\"id\": \"fldAcctNote001\", \"name\": \"Notes\", \"type\": \"multilineText\", \"description\": \"Relationship context, key contacts, and account notes\"}]", "base_id": "appMoveOpsOps001", "description": "Maps client companies to their assigned MoveOps account managers with relationship details"}

{"id": "engagement_vectral_april_2026_wave", "body": "April 2026 Relocation Wave — 3 New Active Moves + Significant Challenges

This is the most intricate set of relocations we've received from Vectral Systems so far. Three relocations occurring concurrently, each with unique challenges that necessitated additional fees and special coordination. Summary below.

**1. Priya Suresh (Senior Data Engineer) — Portland, OR → Denver, CO | April 14–18**
- Pet arrangements needed: Priya is moving with her emotional support German Shepherd, Atlas (85 lbs, ESA documentation available). The new housing must be pet-friendly with a fenced yard or nearby dog park — the landlord must explicitly allow dogs over 85 lbs. Preferred areas: RiNo (top choice) or Highlands.
- Pet Relocation Coordination Fee applied ($350).
- Assigned coordinator: Swift Relocations.

**2. Terrence Nakamura (Staff Platform Architect) — Austin, TX → San Francisco, CA | April 16–22**
- ADA-compliant housing needed: Wheelchair user since 2019. Requires a roll-in shower, doorways of at least 36 inches, and either ground floor or building with an elevator. Preferred neighborhoods in SF: SoMa or Mission Bay, near BART/Muni.
- Split-shipment necessary: Estimated household weight of 9,200 lbs exceeds the standard single-truck capacity. Two vendor legs arranged — Heartland Movers handling the main household items (~6,000 lbs, Shipment 1) and Swift Relocations managing the overflow (~3,200 lbs, Shipment 2) which includes a 150 lb server rack (sensitive equipment, padded crating needed) and around 40 boxes of books.
- ADA Housing Sourcing Premium ($400) + Split-Shipment Fee ($900) applied.
- **Precedent note:** This split-shipment for the Nakamura relocation is the first for Vectral. Considering their engineering team frequently has heavy home-office and lab equipment, this may set a standard for future Vectral moves. It’s worth discussing with operations whether to incorporate split-shipment into Vectral's standard offerings or maintain it as an additional fee.

**3. Camille Oduya (Principal Product Manager) — Boston, MA → Seattle, WA | April 12–21**
- Tight schedule: Boston lease must be vacated by April 12 (fixed date), Seattle office starts April 21 — only 9 days for a cross-country relocation. Movers need to pick up on April 12; household items must reach Seattle by April 16. Vectral has already secured the Seattle apartment (1422 Minor Ave, Apt 7B) — no apartment search needed on our part, just flight + movers. **Priority scheduling required.**
- Enhanced stipend exception: Vectral has arranged a $3,500 stipend for home-office equipment for Camille (as opposed to the standard $2,000) as part of her offer. This needs to be set up in the stipend platform before her arrival on April 14. Alerting finance/operations to ensure this is completed on time.

**Financials:** Invoice INV-2026-0410 issued, total $15,900. Due 2026-05-10.

**Account context:** This raises Vectral's total active relocations to 5 (Kevin Tran and Danielle Osei from the previous wave are still in process). - April 8, 2:28pm: Sam followed up with addresses, building restrictions, and logistics details. Key items: Nadia's building super requires 48-hr notice and weekdays-only moves; Diego wants a pre-move crating inspection (April 10–11); PivotPoint's FY ends April 30 so they need the invoice dated before then.

1. H-1B Visa Timing (Nadia): Her visa expires June 15, 2026. USCIS address-change filing MUST happen before physical move. Attorney Lorraine Pham (Pham & Associates) is expecting our outreach. If filing is delayed, move date slips — tight window.
2. CNC Mill Freight (Diego): 600-lb Tormach PCNC 440 is a first for MoveOps in terms of precision industrial equipment. Requires specialty crating, climate transport, and high-value cargo insurance. Sets a potential precedent for future 'specialty equipment relocation' service offerings.
3. Storage Gap (Diego): 16-day gap between Brooklyn lease end (April 15) and Austin apartment availability (May 1). Climate-controlled storage with dock access needed. Chloe Vance checking SecureStore Austin availability.
4. Pre-Move Inspection (Diego): Employee wants to personally supervise CNC crating — need to coordinate specific time window April 10–11 before the April 12–15 pickup window.

- Diego Castañeda → Javier Morales (heavy equipment documentation), vendor: Heartland Movers

**Account Observations:**
Sam Delgado continues to be very hands-on — providing detailed specs, addresses, building restrictions, and even equipment insurance values upfront. Trust level with MoveOps is growing. {"id": "eng_northwind_denver_recovery_context", "body": "Account Recovery Context -- NorthWind Technologies Denver Satellite Office Expansion

NorthWind Technologies (company_northwind) has requested 2 new employee relocations as part of opening a Denver satellite office. This is a significant moment for the account relationship.

BACKGROUND:
NorthWind signed a $60,000 contract in January 2026 for 5 enterprise relocations at $12K each. Execution was poor: two Phase 1 apartments failed quality standards (HVAC malfunction in Austin, misrepresented listing in Phoenix), and my response time to Pam Kowalski's complaint was unacceptably slow. Pam escalated directly to David Chen (Customer Engagement Lead) citing unresponsive account management and quality concerns. David personally intervened and committed to service improvements. NorthWind's Q1 satisfaction survey came back at 5/10 overall, 4/10 on communication quality, 5/10 on service quality.

Despite this history, Pam Kowalski (Director of HR Operations) has come back to MoveOps for the Denver expansion. She explicitly stated in her email: "NorthWind is giving you a second chance here and I expect better service this time." Victor Huang (CISO) also expressed skepticism, writing that he is "not fully comfortable delegating this to a relocation vendor we've had quality issues with."

CURRENT REQUEST:
1) Raj Patel (Principal Architect) -- Chicago to Denver, week of April 21. Server lab with 4 rack-mounted servers, lithium UPS batteries (hazmat Class 9). CISO requires: chain-of-custody log (signed, timestamped), IT security escort supervision during packing/loading, direct transport only, real-time GPS tracking. This chain-of-custody protocol is unprecedented for MoveOps -- we are developing the template and process from scratch while executing it for a skeptical CISO. Assigned to Swift Relocations.

2) Emilia Cruz (Director of Marketing) -- Chicago to Denver, hard deadline April 18. Steinway Model B grand piano requiring specialty subcontractor (Gentle Giant Piano Movers, no existing vendor agreement). Three-vendor coordination. 27-day lease overlap with early termination negotiation requested (outside MoveOps standard scope). Assigned to Heartland Movers (household goods).

RISKS:
- Chain-of-custody is a completely new service -- no template, no prior experience, unknown whether Swift will accept supervised loading terms
- Piano subcontractor (Gentle Giant) has no vendor agreement, no insurance verification, no rate sheet -- onboarding a new vendor for a high-stakes account
- Lease negotiation is outside standard scope -- unclear if MoveOps should offer this or refer to a tenant's rights attorney
- Invoice at $14,700 ($7,350/move avg) is below original $12K/move enterprise rate -- pricing may need revision
- Two invoice line items are provisional and pending vendor confirmation

OPPORTUNITY:
If we execute well on both relocations, we recover the NorthWind relationship and position MoveOps for additional satellite office moves. The chain-of-custody capability could also become a differentiating service line for tech companies relocating employees with proprietary equipment. NorthWind has 150 employees and is actively expanding -- this is a growth account if we can earn back their trust.

{"id": "engagement_mosaic_damage_incident_041526", "body": "DAMAGE INCIDENT LOG -- Zara Okoye Relocation, Mosaic Robotics
Logged by: Emeka Diallo (Account Manager) on April 15, 2026, 5:45 PM PT

INCIDENT OVERVIEW:
Robotic arm prototype (Unit #1, $90K value) arrived damaged at Atlanta destination following Savannah-to-Atlanta move coordinated by MoveOps on April 14-15, 2026. Damage includes cracked servo motor housing and joint calibration misalignment. Move was handled by Heartland Movers under work order HM-WO-04142026-OKOYE, coordinated by Blessing Okafor.

CHAIN OF CUSTODY TIMELINE:
April 14, 7:30 AM -- Heartland crew (crew lead: Marcus Delaney) arrives at 1247 Abercorn St, Apt 3B, Savannah, GA for packing. Work order specified 4-inch minimum anti-vibration polyethylene foam on all faces.
April 14, ~9:45 AM -- Blessing Okafor countersigns packing checklist REMOTELY via digital signature. She was physically in Philadelphia coordinating Tyler Okonkwo's Canopy Health move (Philadelphia to Denver) and did not visually verify crating materials or foam density.
April 14, ~10:00 AM -- Loading completed. Two Pelican cases + control unit on Heartland truck (designated climate-controlled, 60-80F).
April 14-15 -- Transit via I-16 West to I-75 North (~250 mi). Overnight stop or depot transfer status unconfirmed.
April 15, 9:15 AM -- Delivery at Atlanta address. Receipt signed by Kyle Britten (Mosaic Robotics intern). Zara Okoye was at Mosaic Atlanta office for orientation. No inspection performed at time of delivery.
April 15, ~1:00 PM -- Zara returns, inspects prototypes. Discovers cracked servo housing on Unit #1 (3-inch fracture, wiring harness exposed) and 4+ degree calibration drift on two axes.
April 15, 1:28 PM -- Zara emails Tamara Byrd internally reporting damage, CC's Rafael Mendes.
April 15, 3:42 PM -- Tamara Byrd emails Emeka Diallo (MoveOps) formally escalating. Demands incident report and damage assessment within 48 hours. Rafael Mendes CC'd.
April 15, 4:14 PM -- Emeka acknowledges and commits to 48-hour deadline. CC's Chloe Vance (MoveOps Ops Manager).

KEY FINDINGS:
1. CRATING VERIFICATION GAP: Coordinator Blessing Okafor was managing two same-day pack-and-load operations in Savannah and Philadelphia simultaneously. She signed off on Heartland's packing checklist remotely based on verbal confirmation from crew lead Marcus Delaney, without physically verifying foam density against the 4-inch minimum spec. Blessing has acknowledged this was a procedural failure on her part and has offered to provide her full packing supervision log.
2. UNAUTHORIZED DELIVERY RECIPIENT: Delivery was accepted and signed for by Kyle Britten, a Mosaic Robotics intern, not by Zara Okoye (the authorized recipient). This creates a ~3.75-hour gap between delivery and inspection during which damage provenance cannot be conclusively established. Heartland's insurance adjusters could use this to challenge the claim.
3. INSURANCE COVERAGE GAP (flagged by Marcus Thorne, Head of Finance): MoveOps' vendor liability policy covers $50,000 per item maximum. The damaged unit is valued at $90,000. This creates a $40,000 uncovered gap representing MoveOps' direct financial exposure if Heartland does not accept liability.

FINANCIAL IMPACT:
- Damaged prototype value: $90,000
- Vendor liability coverage: $50,000
- MoveOps direct exposure: $40,000 (worst case without Heartland acceptance)
- QuickBooks credit memo: CM-2026-0415 (against INV-2026-0411)
- QuickBooks liability accrual: ACCRUAL-2026-0415-MOSAIC ($90K contingent)
- Revenue at risk: $9,010 (full Mosaic April invoice) + ongoing account relationship

ACCOUNT RELATIONSHIP STATUS: AT RISK
Mosaic Robotics is a new client (~2 months). The damaged equipment is part of an active DARPA-adjacent R&D program. VP of Engineering Rafael Mendes is now directly engaged. The incident report is due EOD April 17 and must include full transparency about MoveOps' procedural failures (remote crating sign-off) and the $40K insurance gap.

NEXT STEPS:
1. Chloe Vance compiling final incident timeline with Heartland transit records
2. Incident report to be sent to Tamara Byrd by EOD April 17
3. Marcus Thorne scheduling call with MoveOps insurance broker re: claim process
4. Emeka to approach Heartland Movers re: cost-sharing on uncovered $40K
5. $1,200 crating surcharge billing hold recommended by Marcus pending claim resolution

{"id": "engagement-terraform-replyall-incident", "body": "INCIDENT LOG — Reply All Pricing Leak (April 22, 2026)

On April 22, Catalina Dubois accidentally Reply-All'd an internal pricing strategy email to Rachel Nguyen (VP People Ops, Terraform Digital). The email was intended for the internal pricing discussion thread but was sent to Julian Brooks' forward chain, which had Rachel CC'd.

Information exposed to client:
- MoveOps' 25% margin floor (described as "non-negotiable")
- Tiered volume discount structure: standard for 1-10 moves, 8% at 11-20, 12% at 21+
- Internal assessment that Terraform would likely push for 15-20% discount
- Discussion about whether to present pricing proactively vs. reactively

Rachel's response: Professional but firm. She immediately locked in the tiered discount structure as her expectation and noted she'd "learned more about MoveOps' pricing from an accidental email than from any deliberate communication." She also flagged that she still did not have a named AM at that point.

Resolution actions taken:
1. Catalina sent personal apology to Rachel within 1 hour
2. Elena Rostova assigned Mina Hashimoto as dedicated AM (best margin track record, zero escalations)
3. Elena authorized hiring a dedicated Terraform relocation coordinator
4. Pricing decision: honor tiered structure Rachel saw — lead with 8% tier for 11-20 moves; do NOT proactively offer 12% tier
5. Mina sent professional introduction email same day; Rachel responded next morning with full relocation details for 3 employees

Current status: Relationship stabilized. Rachel is engaged and providing relocation details. Monitoring closely — Rachel indicated she is "watching closely" to ensure execution matches intention.

{"folder": "INBOX", "sender": "chloe.vance@moveops.com", "cc_json": "["lena.bjorkstrom@moveops.com"]", "content": "Hi Samira,

The timing for this project is perfect — the expense review process is currently the most time-consuming aspect of our workflow. Here’s all the information you’ll need.

Standard Expense Categories

These are the categories we apply to all clients (though some clients may not cover all of them):

1. Equipment — laptops, monitors, keyboards, mice, webcams, docking stations, cables/adapters
2. Furniture — desks, non-ergonomic chairs, bookshelves, filing cabinets
3. Ergonomic — ergonomic chairs, standing desks, monitor arms, keyboard trays, wrist rests, footrests. This category is separate from general furniture because many clients have higher limits for ergonomic items.
4. Internet — setup fees for home internet, monthly service, mesh wifi systems, ethernet cables
5. Coworking — memberships for coworking spaces, day passes, meeting room rentals
6. Office Supplies — stationery, printer ink, paper, whiteboards, desk organizers
7. Travel — flights for team offsites or client visits (NOT for relocation, which is handled differently)

Common Edge Cases

These are the situations that often cause confusion:

- Bundled items: If someone purchases a 'keyboard + mouse combo' for $120, should it count as one equipment expense or two? We currently categorize it as one.
- Overlap between Ergonomic and Furniture: A standing desk could fit into either category. We place items marketed as 'ergonomic' or 'adjustable height' into the ergonomic category, which typically has a higher limit.
- Coworking memberships: These are usually recurring monthly. Some clients cover them, while others do not. The complexity arises when a client covers day passes but not monthly memberships.
- Internet setup versus monthly service: The first month of internet service often includes an installation fee ($100-200) along with the first month’s service. Some clients only reimburse for monthly service, excluding setup.
- Ergonomic items exceeding $500: Several clients set a cap for ergonomic items at $500 or $600. Employees frequently submit claims for ergonomic chairs priced between $450-550, which often leads to disputes.
- Monitor + arm combinations: A $400 monitor is categorized as equipment, while a $150 monitor arm is considered ergonomic. When these are purchased together as a bundle, categorization can become complicated.

Pilot Client Policies — Vectral Systems vs. Mosaic Robotics

These two clients are excellent candidates for piloting due to their significantly different policies:

Vectral Systems
- Equipment: up to $500 per item, receipts required
- Furniture: up to $400 per item, receipts required
- Ergonomic: up to $600 per item, receipts required (they increased this limit last quarter based on employee feedback)
- Internet: up to $150/month, receipts needed for setup only
- Coworking: NOT COVERED — explicitly excluded in their contract
- Office Supplies: up to $75 per item, no receipt required for items under $25
- Travel: handled separately, not included
- Contact: Rachel Whitfield (HR), collaborates with Mina from our side

Mosaic Robotics
- Equipment: up to $1,000 per item, receipts required for amounts over $200
- Furniture: up to $800 per item, receipts required
- Ergonomic: up to $800 per item (same limit as furniture — they do not differentiate)
- Internet: up to $200/month, receipts necessary
- Coworking: NOT COVERED — they maintain their own satellite offices and do not reimburse for coworking
- Office Supplies: up to $100 per item, no receipt required for items under $50
- Travel: handled separately
- Contact: Priya Delgado (Operations), works with Emeka from our side

⚠️ Important note on Mosaic: six months ago, they revised their coworking policy. They previously covered coworking up to $400/month but discontinued this when they opened satellite offices. Ensure the system reflects the current policy, not the outdated one. I’ve noticed operations team members mistakenly approving coworking expenses for Mosaic based on old information.

General Recommendations

- The option to 'flag for review' is crucial — when uncertain, DO NOT auto-deny. A wrongful rejection is more problematic than a delayed approval from the client's perspective. Employees become frustrated when legitimate expenses are denied.
- We need the capability for quick overrides. Currently, if an expense is incorrectly denied, it takes 1-2 days to resolve because it’s handled via email.
- Policy configurations should be easy to update. Clients tend to change their policies approximately once or twice a year, and we must be able to reflect those changes quickly.

I’ve attached the complete policy spreadsheet with all current clients (not just the two pilot cases). Let me know if you need anything else — I’m happy to discuss edge cases over a call.

Best,  
Chloe", "is_read": true, "subject": "Re: Expense auto-categorization project — need your input on categories & client policies", "email_id": "email_ops_requirements", "parent_id": "email_arch_kickoff_to_ops", "timestamp": "2026-02-05T17:15:00+00:00", "recipients_json": "["samira.tariq@moveops.com"]", "attachments_json": "{"client_expense_policies_2026.xlsx": {"size": 48200, "type": "application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet"}}"}

{"folder": "SENT", "sender": "samira.tariq@moveops.com", "cc_json": "["julian.brooks@moveops.com", "lena.bjorkstrom@moveops.com", "alejandro.fuentes@moveops.com"]", "content": "Marcus, Elena,

Here is the comprehensive root cause analysis for the AWS cost surge in March.

Summary
The AWS costs surged threefold ($42,870 compared to a baseline of approximately $14,300) due to the onboarding of Vantage Distributed enterprise, which started on March 21 and included over 1,200 concurrent users.

Root Cause Details
- Trigger: On March 21, Vantage Distributed initiated self-service onboarding for 1,047 employees and around 180 contractors.
- API Traffic: The self-service portal's batch imports led to consistently high API traffic for more than seven days.
- Autoscaling: The EC2 autoscaling group increased from 4 to 18 instances due to CPU usage exceeding the 80% threshold.
- Storage: There were 47,000 document uploads to S3 within a week, compared to the usual 2,000 per month.
- Database: The average connection count rose from 50 to 340, accompanied by slow queries related to the relocation matching algorithm.

Cost Breakdown
- EC2 Compute: $28,450
- S3 Storage & Transfer: $5,500
- RDS Database: $8,920

Assessment
This was a genuine customer-driven surge; our infrastructure scaled appropriately. The challenge lies in a pricing model gap: we failed to account for per-employee infrastructure costs when establishing the pricing for this agreement. The expenses related to onboarding were not foreseen or included in the budget.

Recommended Actions
1. Immediate: Set up AWS cost alert thresholds (50%, 100%, 200% of the baseline).
2. Short-term: Develop an infrastructure cost estimator for sales to use during pricing discussions.
3. Medium-term: Reassess and revise the enterprise pricing model to incorporate per-employee infrastructure tiers.
4. Ongoing: Conduct monthly cost review meetings involving both engineering and finance teams.

I accept responsibility for not raising the early warning signs sooner. I observed increased metrics on March 28 but waited for the invoice to raise the concern. That delay is my oversight.

I’m open to further discussion and available all week.

{"folder": "SENT", "sender": "emeka.diallo@moveops.com", "cc_json": "["rafael.mendes@mosaicrobotics.com", "chloe.vance@moveops.com"]", "content": "Tamara,

As promised in my initial response on April 15, please find below the complete incident report and damage assessment for the prototype damage sustained during Zara Okoye's relocation from Savannah, GA to Atlanta, GA. This report is being delivered within the 48-hour window you requested.

I want to state at the outset that MoveOps accepts responsibility for the process failures that contributed to this incident. This report is intended to give you and Rafael full transparency into what happened, what went wrong on our end, and what we are doing to make this right.


SECTION 1: CHAIN OF CUSTODY TIMELINE

April 14, 7:30 AM -- Heartland Movers crew (crew lead: Marcus Delaney, plus 2 additional movers) arrived at 1247 Abercorn St, Apt 3B, Savannah, GA to begin packing. Per MoveOps work order HM-WO-04142026-OKOYE, Heartland was instructed to use anti-vibration polyethylene foam crating at a minimum 4-inch density on all six interior faces of each Pelican case, with custom-fitted internal foam cradles for the articulated arm assemblies, and a separate padded compartment for the control unit.

April 14, approximately 9:45 AM -- MoveOps Relocation Coordinator Blessing Okafor countersigned Heartland's packing completion checklist via remote digital signature. Ms. Okafor was not physically present at the Savannah address during packing. She was at a separate job site in Philadelphia, PA coordinating a concurrent relocation for another MoveOps client. She verified packing completion based on a verbal confirmation from Heartland crew lead Delaney via telephone. The foam density and crating materials were not physically inspected by any MoveOps personnel before the cases were sealed.

April 14, approximately 10:00 AM -- Loading completed. Two Pelican 1780 cases containing prototype units #1 and #2, and one Pelican 1620 case containing the control unit, were placed on a Heartland Movers truck designated for climate-controlled transport (60-80 degrees Fahrenheit, per work order specification).

April 14-15, Transit -- The truck departed Savannah via I-16 West to I-75 North, a route of approximately 250 miles. Heartland's dispatch records confirm the truck made an unscheduled overnight stop at Heartland's Macon, GA depot on the evening of April 14. The driver resumed transit the morning of April 15. MoveOps has formally requested temperature logs and depot handling records from Heartland for the Macon stop. These records have not yet been provided. This overnight stop was not part of the original transit plan -- a 250-mile route is within same-day delivery range.

April 15, 9:15 AM -- Delivery at the Atlanta destination address. The delivery receipt was signed by Kyle Britten, who has been identified as a Mosaic Robotics intern. Zara Okoye was not present at the time of delivery -- she was at the Mosaic Atlanta office for new employee orientation. No external or internal inspection of the packages was performed at the time of delivery. The Heartland driver's notes state "delivered to building resident, no issues noted" but this notation reflects only the exterior condition of the shipping containers, not the contents.

April 15, approximately 1:00 PM -- Zara Okoye returned to the apartment and inspected the prototypes. She discovered the following damage to Prototype Unit #1:

(a) The servo motor housing on the primary joint assembly is cracked. The fracture runs approximately 3 inches along the lateral face of the housing and has exposed the internal wiring harness. The unit cannot be safely powered on in its current state.

(b) The joint calibration has shifted by more than 4 degrees on two axes, as confirmed by Zara's onboard encoder diagnostic readouts. This magnitude of calibration drift is consistent with the unit having been subjected to significant impact shock or sustained vibration against a hard surface during transport.

(c) Zara also observed that the foam crating around Unit #1 appeared thinner than the foam around Unit #2, suggesting the crating may not have been applied uniformly across both cases.

Prototype Unit #2 and the control unit showed no visible external damage at the time of Zara's inspection. We understand Mosaic's engineering team is scheduling a full diagnostic for both units within the coming days.


SECTION 2: ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFICATION

The primary root cause of this incident is a failure in MoveOps' crating verification process.

Our standard protocol for high-value item transport requires that the assigned MoveOps coordinator be physically present at the packing site to inspect and verify that the vendor's crating materials meet the specifications documented in the work order. In this case, the work order explicitly required 4-inch minimum density anti-vibration polyethylene foam. That specification exists precisely because of the sensitivity of the servo motors and calibration assemblies in equipment like Zara's prototypes.

On April 14, our coordinator was managing two same-day packing operations in two different cities -- Zara Okoye's move in Savannah, GA and a separate client relocation in Philadelphia, PA. Rather than requesting a second coordinator to provide physical oversight in Savannah, she attempted to manage both remotely and signed off on Heartland's packing checklist based on verbal confirmation from the crew lead without visual verification.

This means MoveOps cannot confirm whether Heartland Movers actually used the 4-inch minimum density foam specified in the work order. Zara's observation that the foam around Unit #1 appeared thinner than around Unit #2 is consistent with either substandard foam being used or inconsistent application between the two cases.

MoveOps takes full ownership of this process failure. The coordinator's decision to sign off remotely was a direct deviation from our own protocol for high-value cargo. It should not have happened, and we are implementing changes to ensure it cannot happen again (see Section 5).

A contributing factor was the unscheduled overnight stop at Heartland's Macon, GA depot. If the climate control was not maintained during the depot stop, or if the cases were moved or repositioned by depot personnel, this could have contributed to the damage. We are pursuing temperature logs and handling records from Heartland and will share any findings with you as they become available.


SECTION 3: DELIVERY RECEIPT DISCLOSURE

For the sake of complete transparency, we want to note that the delivery receipt at the Atlanta address was signed by Kyle Britten, a Mosaic Robotics intern, rather than by Zara Okoye.

MoveOps is not raising this fact to deflect or disclaim responsibility. We believe the damage to Prototype Unit #1 most likely occurred during packing or transit, not after delivery. The pattern of damage -- cracked servo housing and multi-axis calibration drift -- is consistent with transport-related shock or vibration, not post-delivery mishandling.

We are noting this for the record because it will be relevant to the insurance adjudication process. Heartland Movers' standard terms include a provision requiring damage to be reported at time of delivery if the authorized recipient is present to inspect. Since the authorized recipient was not present, there is a procedural gap that Heartland's insurance adjusters may reference. We want you to be aware of this so that Meridian Risk Partners can factor it into your filing strategy.

To be clear: MoveOps does not believe the identity of the delivery signer changes who is at fault for the damage. We are not using this to limit our accountability.


SECTION 4: INSURANCE CLAIM STATUS AND FINANCIAL DISCLOSURE

MoveOps has initiated the insurance claim process on our end. Here is the current status:

(a) A credit memo (CM-2026-0415) has been applied against your invoice INV-2026-0411 to reflect the pending insurance claim for the damaged prototype.

(b) MoveOps' vendor liability insurance policy covers a maximum of $50,000 per item for third-party transported goods.

(c) Prototype Unit #1 is valued at $90,000, which means there is a $40,000 gap between our policy coverage and the full value of the damaged unit.

I want to be upfront about this gap rather than have it surface later in the insurance process. MoveOps is actively pursuing recovery of the uncovered $40,000 from Heartland Movers, as the damage occurred while the prototype was in their custody and under their handling. We believe the unverified crating and the unscheduled Macon depot stop support a strong case for Heartland liability. Our Head of Finance, Marcus Thorne, is also scheduling a call with our insurance broker this week to explore all avenues for closing the coverage gap.

We will keep you and Meridian Risk Partners informed as the adjudication process progresses. MoveOps is committed to ensuring Mosaic Robotics is made whole on this claim.

Additionally, we have placed a billing hold on the $1,200 custom crating surcharge that was included on invoice INV-2026-0411 for Zara's move. We will not bill Mosaic for crating services while the adequacy of those crating services is under investigation. The remaining balance on INV-2026-0411 for the other two April relocations (Deshawn Hartley and Keiko Tanaka) is unaffected.


SECTION 5: PROPOSED REMEDIATION

MoveOps proposes the following remediation measures:

(a) Replacement Prototype Transport at MoveOps' Cost: When Mosaic Robotics is ready to ship a replacement prototype to Zara in Atlanta, MoveOps will coordinate and fully fund the transport. The replacement shipment will use a dedicated climate-controlled vehicle (not a shared truck), and a senior MoveOps operations manager will be physically present for the entire process from packing through delivery. There will be no remote sign-offs and no shared scheduling with other moves.

(b) Full Cooperation with Insurance: MoveOps will provide all documentation requested by Meridian Risk Partners for Mosaic's insurance filing, including our internal work orders, the Heartland packing checklist, coordinator logs, delivery receipts, and this incident report.


SECTION 6: PROCESS IMPROVEMENTS

Effective immediately, MoveOps is implementing the following policy changes:

(a) Mandatory In-Person Crating Verification: For any item valued at $10,000 or above, a MoveOps coordinator must be physically present at the packing site to visually inspect and photograph the crating materials before cases are sealed. Remote digital sign-offs on packing checklists will no longer be accepted for high-value cargo under any circumstances.

(b) No Concurrent High-Value Assignments: Coordinators will not be assigned to multiple same-day packing operations if any of the moves involve items valued above $10,000. If scheduling conflicts arise, a second coordinator will be assigned or the move will be rescheduled.

(c) Authorized Recipient Verification at Delivery: For high-value items, MoveOps will require that the named recipient (or a pre-authorized designee approved in writing by the client) be present at delivery to perform an immediate inspection and sign the receipt. If the authorized party is unavailable, the delivery will be held until they are present.

(d) Vendor Transit Documentation Requirements: For moves involving items valued above $25,000, MoveOps will require real-time GPS tracking and continuous temperature logging from the transport vendor. These records will be provided to the client within 24 hours of delivery.


Tamara, I understand the gravity of this situation. Mosaic Robotics entrusted MoveOps with a $90,000 piece of R&D equipment on an active development program, and we fell short of the standard we committed to. I am personally accountable for the outcome on this account, and I will ensure that every remediation measure described above is executed.

I am available to discuss this report by phone at any time. If you and Rafael would prefer a video call to walk through the findings and next steps together, I am happy to arrange that as well. Please let me know what works best for your team.

Respectfully,

Emeka Diallo
Account Manager
MoveOps Inc.

This letter serves as formal notification that the U.S. Department of Transportation, Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration (PHMSA), Hazardous Materials Division, is conducting a compliance inquiry regarding MoveOps Inc. (EIN on file, registered business address: 548 Market St, Suite 310, San Francisco, CA 94104) in its capacity as a shipper of record for hazardous materials transported in commerce during Q1-Q2 of calendar year 2026.

CASE NUMBER: PHMSA-2026-04-1187

BACKGROUND

This inquiry was initiated as a result of a routine inspection of carrier records maintained by Swift Relocations LLC (USDOT No. 3281074, MC-927451). During the inspection, PHMSA field investigators identified MoveOps Inc. as the shipper of record on multiple hazardous materials shipping documents associated with shipments coordinated through Swift Relocations during the January 1, 2026 through June 30, 2026 period. The nature of the materials identified in these records — including but not limited to Class 9 lithium-ion battery modules and Class 3B CO2 laser equipment — indicates that these shipments fall under the regulatory purview of the Hazardous Materials Regulations (HMR), 49 CFR Parts 171 through 180.

DOCUMENTS REQUESTED

Pursuant to 49 U.S.C. 5121 and 49 CFR Part 107, Subpart F, MoveOps Inc. is hereby requested to produce the following documentation for ALL hazardous materials shipments coordinated, arranged, or facilitated by MoveOps Inc. during the period of January 1, 2026 through June 30, 2026:

1. Shipping Papers — Complete copies of all hazardous materials shipping papers prepared in accordance with 49 CFR Part 172, Subpart C, including proper shipping names, hazard class designations, UN identification numbers, packing group assignments, and quantity descriptions for each shipment.

2. Hazmat Placarding Certificates — Documentation confirming that appropriate placards were affixed to transport vehicles in compliance with 49 CFR Part 172, Subpart F (Sections 172.500 through 172.560), including signed placarding certificates or equivalent verification records for each shipment.

3. Carrier Certifications — Copies of carrier hazardous materials registration certificates (per 49 CFR Part 107, Subpart G) for all motor carriers utilized by MoveOps Inc. for the transport of hazardous materials during the inquiry period, confirming that each carrier maintained valid PHMSA registration at the time of transport.

4. Emergency Response Documentation — Copies of emergency response information provided to carriers in accordance with 49 CFR Part 172, Subpart G (Section 172.602), including emergency response telephone numbers maintained during transport.

5. Training Records — Documentation demonstrating that MoveOps Inc. personnel involved in the offering of hazardous materials for transportation received hazmat training and testing as required under 49 CFR Part 172, Subpart H (Sections 172.700 through 172.704).

RESPONSE DEADLINE

MoveOps Inc. is required to submit the above-referenced documentation within thirty (30) calendar days of receipt of this letter. Based on the date of transmission, the response deadline is May 21, 2026.

    Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration
    U.S. Department of Transportation
    East Building, 2nd Floor (PHH-32)
    1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
    Washington, DC 20590

    Electronic submissions: hazmat.compliance@dot.gov

POTENTIAL CONSEQUENCES OF NON-COMPLIANCE

Please be advised that failure to respond to this inquiry within the specified timeframe, or submission of incomplete or inaccurate documentation, may result in enforcement action pursuant to 49 U.S.C. 5123, including civil penalties of up to $59,017 per violation per day (as adjusted for inflation per 49 CFR 107.329). Each shipment for which documentation is incomplete or missing may constitute a separate violation.

MoveOps Inc. is encouraged to review its records thoroughly and to contact this office with any questions regarding the scope of this inquiry. If you require clarification on any of the requested documentation categories, please contact me directly at the number below.

{"folder": "INBOX", "sender": "marcus.thorne@moveops.com", "cc_json": "[]", "content": "David,

Good that you're getting ahead of this — pricing on enterprise-scale accounts is where deals like this get made or broken.

I want to anchor the conversation before Alejandro runs his models: our margin floor is 25%. That is non-negotiable. We learned this the hard way during some of our early client deals where we priced too aggressively to win volume and ended up underwater once vendor costs, coordinator time, and operational overhead were factored in.

A few things to keep in mind as you think about discount tiers:

Our blended margin on relocations currently sits around 32-35%, depending on the city pair and move complexity. That margin includes apartment sourcing, flight booking, and moving coordination — it does NOT include the stipend platform fee, which is a separate recurring revenue line at $2,000/month for Terraform.

At 10% off our standard rate, we'd be looking at roughly 25-28% margin depending on volume. That's tight but above the floor. At 15%, we're almost certainly below 25% once you factor in the incremental coordinator costs that come with higher volume — more moves means more vendor management overhead, more scheduling conflicts, more things that can go wrong (ask Emeka about the Mosaic prototype).

My recommendation: do NOT lead with a discount. Let Rachel ask. If she pushes, start at 8% and hold firm. We can go to 10% maximum with Elena's sign-off, but 15% is off the table unless the deal structure changes (e.g., longer contract commitment, minimum volume guarantee).

{"folder": "INBOX", "sender": "alejandro.fuentes@moveops.com", "cc_json": "[]", "content": "David, Marcus, all,

I ran the numbers on the three scenarios David outlined, plus a tiered structure I think makes more sense for an account like this. Including the per-move operational overhead Marcus flagged (~$225 average).

BASELINE ASSUMPTIONS:
Standard relocation rate: $2,500 per move
Blended COGS per move (vendor costs + coordinator time): $1,625
Operational overhead per move: $225
Blended cost basis: $1,850 per move
Gross margin at standard rate: $650/move = 26% after overhead
(Note: our 35% margin figure excludes coordinator overhead — once you add Marcus's $225 estimate, effective margin at standard rate is closer to 26-27%. I'm using the fully loaded number below.)

Wait — let me correct that. Looking at our actual P&L data from Q1, the coordinator overhead is partially absorbed in our fixed salary costs, not variable per-move. The variable overhead is closer to $125/move for scheduling, vendor comms, and QA. So:

Revised cost basis: $1,750 per move
Gross margin at standard rate: $750/move = 30%

SCENARIO MODELING (using revised cost basis):

Scenario 1: Standard rates, no discount ($2,500/move)
  3 relocations/quarter: $7,500 revenue, $2,250 margin (30%)
  10 relocations/quarter: $25,000 revenue, $7,500 margin (30%)
  20 relocations/quarter: $50,000 revenue, $15,000 margin (30%)
  30 relocations/quarter: $75,000 revenue, $22,500 margin (30%)

Scenario 2: 10% volume discount ($2,250/move)
  3 relocations/quarter: $6,750 revenue, $1,500 margin (22%) -- BELOW FLOOR
  10 relocations/quarter: $22,500 revenue, $5,000 margin (22%) -- BELOW FLOOR
  20 relocations/quarter: $45,000 revenue, $10,000 margin (22%) -- BELOW FLOOR

Hmm, that's not right either. Let me redo this properly.

Actually, I realize the issue — David, when you said 35% margin, I think you're using the revenue-minus-vendor-cost figure without overhead. Let me reconcile with Marcus's numbers. Marcus said blended margin is 32-35%. If I take 33% as the midpoint:

At $2,500/move with 33% margin: cost basis = $1,675
  10% discount ($2,250): margin = ($2,250 - $1,675) / $2,250 = 25.6% -- JUST above floor
  15% discount ($2,125): margin = ($2,125 - $1,675) / $2,125 = 21.2% -- BELOW floor

OK, so here's the clean summary using 33% baseline margin (cost basis $1,675/move):

Standard ($2,500/move): 33% margin
  20 moves/qtr = $50,000 revenue, $16,500 margin

10% discount ($2,250/move): 25.6% margin -- barely above 25% floor
  20 moves/qtr = $45,000 revenue, $11,500 margin

15% discount ($2,125/move): 21.2% margin -- BELOW 25% floor
  20 moves/qtr = $42,500 revenue, $9,000 margin

At 15%, we are definitively below the margin floor at every volume level. Marcus is right that 15% is a non-starter.

At 10%, we're technically above the floor but there's zero cushion. One complex move (think Raj Patel's server lab or the Mosaic prototype situation) and we're underwater on that individual relocation.

MY RECOMMENDATION: Tiered structure that rewards volume while protecting margin

Tier 1 (1-10 moves/quarter): Standard rate, no discount -- $2,500/move, 33% margin
Tier 2 (11-20 moves/quarter): 8% discount -- $2,300/move, 27.2% margin (above floor with buffer)
Tier 3 (21+ moves/quarter): 12% discount -- $2,200/move, 23.9% margin

I know Tier 3 technically dips below 25%, but the logic is: if Terraform is sending us 21+ moves per quarter, we're looking at $46,200+/quarter in revenue from a single client. The absolute margin dollars at that volume ($11,000+/quarter) more than justify the rate compression, and we should be getting coordinator efficiency gains at that scale that bring real costs down.

For the first 3 relocations, I'd suggest we keep standard rates. Offering a discount on 3 moves makes no sense — volume pricing should kick in when there's actual volume.

One more thing: the $2,000/month stipend platform fee is pure recurring SaaS-like revenue at roughly 85% margin. That cushions the overall Terraform relationship margin significantly. If you blend the stipend revenue with even 20 moves/quarter at Tier 2 pricing, the overall account margin comes to approximately 31%.

Happy to walk anyone through the spreadsheet. I have a more detailed model with sensitivity analysis on city-pair cost variance if Marcus wants it.

Alejandro Fuentes

{"id": "proj_expense_autocategorizer", "name": "Expense Auto-Categorization Engine", "state": "planned", "lead_id": "moveops_samira_tariq", "summary": "Automated expense categorization and approval/denial system to replace manual email-based expense review for client relocations.", "team_id": "team_engineering", "priority": 1, "created_at": "2026-02-01T17:15:00Z", "start_date": "2026-02-03", "updated_at": "2026-02-01T17:15:00Z", "description": "## Problem
Our current expense review process is entirely manual and email-based. AMs receive expense receipts from relocated employees, manually categorize them (equipment, furniture, internet, coworking, etc.), check them against per-client policy limits, and approve/deny via email. This is slow (~3 day turnaround), error-prone (we've had multiple mis-categorizations this quarter), and doesn't scale as we onboard more clients.

## Proposed Solution
Build an auto-categorization engine that:
1. Ingests submitted expense receipts
2. Auto-categorizes by expense type
3. Validates against per-client policy rules (limits, excluded categories)
4. Auto-approves or flags for manual review based on confidence + policy match

## Success Criteria
- Reduce expense review turnaround from ~3 days to <4 hours
- 95%+ categorization accuracy on standard expense types
- Per-client configurable policy rules
- Admin dashboard for manual override and audit trail", "is_archived": false, "labels_json": "["expense-tool", "q1-priority"]", "target_date": "2026-03-27"}

{"id": "issue_category_matcher", "title": "Implement CategoryMatcher module (rules-based, keyword scoring)", "number": 205, "team_id": "team_engineering", "cycle_id": null, "due_date": "2026-02-13", "estimate": 5, "priority": 1, "state_id": "state_todo", "parent_id": null, "created_at": "2026-02-03T17:00:00Z", "creator_id": null, "identifier": null, "links_json": "[]", "project_id": "proj_expense_autocategorizer", "started_at": null, "updated_at": "2026-02-03T17:00:00Z", "archived_at": null, "assignee_id": "moveops_lena_bjorkstrom", "canceled_at": null, "description": "Build the CategoryMatcher component of the auto-categorization pipeline.

## Requirements
- Keyword-based classification with weighted scoring per category
- Categories: `equipment`, `furniture`, `internet`, `ergonomic`, `coworking`, `office_supplies`, `travel`, `other`
- Multi-word phrase matching takes priority over single-keyword matching
- Configurable confidence threshold (default 0.7) — below threshold → `other` + flag for review
- Abstract interface (`ICategoryMatcher`) so we can swap in ML classifier later (v2)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Given a receipt description and amount, returns category + confidence score
- [ ] Multi-word phrases scored higher than single keywords
- [ ] Fallback to `other` when no category exceeds confidence threshold
- [ ] Interface is abstract and implementation-agnostic
- [ ] Unit tests for all standard categories + ambiguous cases

Parent: ENG-201", "is_archived": false, "labels_json": "["expense-tool", "backend"]", "completed_at": null, "relations_json": "[]", "time_in_status_minutes": null}

{"id": "issue_policy_validator", "title": "Implement PolicyValidator — per-client policy rule evaluation", "number": 206, "team_id": "team_engineering", "cycle_id": null, "due_date": "2026-02-20", "estimate": 5, "priority": 1, "state_id": "state_todo", "parent_id": null, "created_at": "2026-02-03T17:05:00Z", "creator_id": null, "identifier": null, "links_json": "[]", "project_id": "proj_expense_autocategorizer", "started_at": null, "updated_at": "2026-02-03T17:05:00Z", "archived_at": null, "assignee_id": "moveops_lena_bjorkstrom", "canceled_at": null, "description": "Build the PolicyValidator that checks categorized expenses against per-client policy configs.

## Requirements
- Load client policy config (JSON) by client_id
- Validate: (a) category is in allowed list, (b) amount ≤ category limit, (c) category not in exclusions
- Return structured result: `{ allowed: bool, reason: string, rule_matched: string }`
- Handle monthly recurring expenses (e.g., internet) vs one-time

## Policy Schema
```json
{
  \"client_id\": \"string\",
  \"categories\": { \"<category>\": { \"max_amount\": number, \"requires_receipt\": bool } },
  \"excluded_categories\": [\"string\"],
  \"default_action\": \"flag_for_review\" | \"deny\"
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Correctly evaluates policy rules for all category types
- [ ] Returns clear denial reason when policy is violated
- [ ] Handles missing categories gracefully (flags for review)
- [ ] Handles excluded categories (auto-deny with reason)
- [ ] Unit tests for each validation path

Parent: ENG-201", variation_level: moderate", "is_archived": false, "labels_json": "["expense-tool", "backend"]", "completed_at": null, "relations_json": "[]", "time_in_status_minutes": null}

{"id": "issue_decision_engine", "title": "Implement DecisionEngine — auto-approve/deny/flag logic", "number": 207, "team_id": "team_engineering", "cycle_id": null, "due_date": "2026-02-27", "estimate": 3, "priority": 1, "state_id": "state_todo", "parent_id": null, "created_at": "2026-02-03T17:10:00Z", "creator_id": null, "identifier": null, "links_json": "[]", "project_id": "proj_expense_autocategorizer", "started_at": null, "updated_at": "2026-02-03T17:10:00Z", "archived_at": null, "assignee_id": "moveops_lena_bjorkstrom", "canceled_at": null, "description": "Build the DecisionEngine that takes CategoryMatcher + PolicyValidator outputs and produces a final decision.

## Decision Logic
- `auto_approve`: category confidence ≥ threshold AND policy check passed AND receipt present
- `auto_deny`: category confidence ≥ threshold AND (exceeds limit OR excluded category)
- `flag_for_review`: category confidence < threshold, missing receipt, or amount within 10% of threshold

## Requirements
- Full decision logging with reasoning chain (category scores, policy rule matched, decision rationale)
- Audit trail entry for every decision
- Support manual override flag (for dashboard integration)

Parent: ENG-201", "is_archived": false, "labels_json": "["expense-tool", "backend"]", "completed_at": null, "relations_json": "[]", "time_in_status_minutes": null}

{"id": "issue_rule_config_ui", "title": "Build rule configuration UI for per-client policy management", "number": 208, "team_id": "team_engineering", "cycle_id": null, "due_date": "2026-02-27", "estimate": 5, "priority": 2, "state_id": "state_todo", "parent_id": null, "created_at": "2026-02-03T17:15:00Z", "creator_id": null, "identifier": null, "links_json": "[]", "project_id": "proj_expense_autocategorizer", "started_at": null, "updated_at": "2026-02-03T17:15:00Z", "archived_at": null, "assignee_id": "moveops_anh_nguyen", "canceled_at": null, "description": "Create a form-based UI for the ops team to manage per-client expense policy configurations without editing raw JSON.

## Features

- Edit form: add/remove categories, set per-category limits, toggle exclusions

- Validation feedback (e.g., 'limit must be > 0', 'at least one category required')

## Notes

{"id": "issue_p1_mosaic_malformed", "title": "[P1] Fix Mosaic Robotics policy config — malformed JSON types causing skipped exclusion checks and amount validation", "number": 215, "team_id": "team_engineering", "cycle_id": null, "due_date": "2026-03-18", "estimate": 5, "priority": 1, "state_id": "state_in_progress", "parent_id": null, "created_at": "2026-03-16T23:45:00Z", "creator_id": null, "identifier": null, "links_json": "[]", "project_id": "proj_expense_autocategorizer", "started_at": null, "updated_at": "2026-03-16T23:45:00Z", "archived_at": null, "assignee_id": "moveops_lena_bjorkstrom", "canceled_at": null, "description": "## Summary
Mosaic Robotics policy config has malformed JSON field types, causing the PolicyValidator to skip exclusion checks and amount comparisons. Results in out-of-policy expenses being auto-approved.

## Root Cause
Two type errors in the generated Mosaic policy config:

### Issue A: `excluded_categories` is a string instead of an array
```json
// Production (malformed)
\"excluded_categories\": \"coworking\"

// Correct
\"excluded_categories\": [\"coworking\"]
```
The PolicyValidator does an `Array.isArray()` check before evaluating exclusions. Since the field is a string, the check fails and the exclusion logic is skipped entirely. The default path is `approve`.

### Issue B: `max_amount` is a string instead of a number
```json
// Production (malformed)
\"ergonomic\": { \"max_amount\": \"800\", \"requires_receipt\": true }

// Correct
\"ergonomic\": { \"max_amount\": 800, \"requires_receipt\": true }
```
The comparison `1200 <= \"800\"` uses JavaScript lexicographic string comparison, where `\"1\" < \"8\"` is true. So the $1,200 expense passes the $800 limit check.

## Impact
- **2 confirmed false approvals** (Andre Washington: $1,200 standing desk, $350/mo coworking membership)
- **Total financial exposure: $1,550+** (coworking may recur monthly)
- Potentially more false approvals if other Mosaic employees submitted out-of-policy expenses
- Mosaic HR (Priya Delgado) not yet aware — quarterly reconciliation in ~6 weeks

## Reproduction Steps
### Issue A (exclusion skip):
1. Load the production Mosaic config
2. Submit a test expense with category `coworking`, any amount
3. Observe PolicyValidator skips exclusion check (Array.isArray returns false for string)
4. Expense is auto-approved despite coworking being excluded

### Issue B (string comparison):
1. Load the production Mosaic config
2. Submit a test expense with category `ergonomic`, amount $1,200
3. Observe `1200 <= \"800\"` evaluates to true (lexicographic)
4. Expense is auto-approved despite exceeding $800 limit

## Fix Required
1. **Immediate:** Fix Mosaic config — correct types for `excluded_categories` (array) and all `max_amount` fields (number)
2. **Pipeline fix:** Add strict type enforcement in ingestion pipeline output — numbers must be numbers, arrays must be arrays
3. **PolicyValidator hardening:** Add runtime type checks before comparisons — `typeof max_amount !== 'number'` should throw, not silently pass
4. **Validation:** Schema validation on config load that rejects malformed types

## Severity
This is more severe than the Vectral bug (ENG-214). False approvals have direct financial impact — money was paid out that shouldn't have been. False rejections are frustrating but recoverable.

## Related
- ENG-209 (ingestion pipeline — source of type errors)
- ENG-212 (edge case tests — would have caught malformed JSON)
- ENG-214 (Vectral config bug — same pipeline, different failure mode)
- ENG-211 (pilot monitoring)", variation_level: moderate", "is_archived": false, "labels_json": "["expense-tool", "bug", "p1", "pilot-incident", "financial-impact"]", "completed_at": null, "relations_json": "[]", "time_in_status_minutes": null}

{"id": "issue_stipend_duplicate_detection", "title": "Stipend duplicate detection — receipt hash fingerprinting & PDF metadata similarity scoring", "number": 220, "team_id": "team_engineering", "cycle_id": null, "due_date": "2026-03-25", "estimate": 5, "priority": 1, "state_id": "state_todo", "parent_id": null, "created_at": "2026-03-15T16:00:00Z", "creator_id": null, "identifier": null, "links_json": "[]", "project_id": "proj_expense_autocategorizer", "started_at": null, "updated_at": "2026-03-15T16:00:00Z", "archived_at": null, "assignee_id": "moveops_dmitri_volkov", "canceled_at": null, "description": "## Background — Audit Findings

In the course of the data quality audit following the pause of ExpenseBot (ENG-216), I identified three questionable stipend submissions that our current processing system handled without conducting any cross-reference checks. The submissions exhibit forensic fingerprints that should have been detected automatically.

### Flagged Transactions

| # | Employee | Client | Item | Vendor | Amount | SHA-256 Hash | PDF Template | Status |
|---|----------|--------|------|--------|--------|-------------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | Kevin Tran | Vectral Systems | Ergonomic keyboard — TechComfort ErgoKey Pro | TechComfort Supply | $89.00 | `a3f7c8d1...f0a2b4` | TC-RECEIPT-STD-041 | Paid (3/8) |
| 2 | Andre Washington | Mosaic Robotics | Ergonomic keyboard — TechComfort ErgoKey Pro | TechComfort Supply | $89.00 | `a3f7c8d1...f0a2b4` | TC-RECEIPT-STD-041 | On Hold |
| 3 | Jordan Ekwueme | BrightLoop Analytics | Monitor stand — TechComfort StandPro Mini | TechComfort Supply | $45.00 | `7b9e1d3f...7e9b` | TC-RECEIPT-STD-041 | On Hold |

**Finding A:** Records #1 and #2 are identical PDF files (same SHA-256 hash). Two employees from different client companies submitted exactly the same receipt file.

**Finding B:** Record #3 is a different file (different hash, different content) but contains identical PDF `/Creator` metadata (`TechComfort Supply Invoice Generator v2.1`) and `/Template` ID (`TC-RECEIPT-STD-041`). Structural analysis reveals matching font metrics, field positioning coordinates, and layout geometry — indicating the same template source.

**Financial exposure:** A total of $223 ($89 already disbursed, $134 pending in finance).

### The Gap

Our current pipeline processes each submission in isolation:

```
ExpenseSubmission → CategoryMatcher → PolicyValidator → DecisionEngine → Approve/Deny/Flag
```

At no stage does any component compare a new submission against historical records. There are:

1. **No receipt hash deduplication** — identical files are processed undetected
2. **No PDF metadata fingerprinting** — receipts generated from the same template are not linked
3. **No cross-client comparison** — a receipt submitted under Client A is never compared with receipts from Client B

This allows someone to submit the same receipt PDF to multiple client accounts, with each instance being assessed independently. If the amounts comply with each client's policy limits, every instance is automatically approved.

---

## Proposed Solution — DuplicateDetector Pipeline Stage

Introduce a new `DuplicateDetector` stage that operates **before** `CategoryMatcher` in the pipeline:

```
ExpenseSubmission → DuplicateDetector → CategoryMatcher → PolicyValidator → DecisionEngine
```

### Layer 1: Exact Hash Match (SHA-256)

- Upon receipt upload/ingestion, compute the SHA-256 hash of the uploaded file
- Save the hash in the receipt metadata record (this is already computed for S3 storage deduplication — refer to Lena's comment in #engineering — but it's not currently used for validation)
- Before processing, check the hash index for any existing matches
- **If an exact match is found:** immediately flag the submission as `DUPLICATE_EXACT` and direct it to the manual review queue. Do NOT auto-approve.
- Include in the flag: the matching record ID, employee name, client, and submission date

### Layer 2: PDF Metadata Similarity Scoring

- At ingestion, extract PDF metadata fields: `/Creator`, `/Producer`, `/Template` (custom), `/CreationDate`
- Generate a metadata fingerprint vector from these fields
- Compare against stored fingerprints using a weighted similarity score:
  - Exact match on `/Template`: +0.5 (strongest indicator — same receipt generator template)
  - Exact match on `/Creator`: +0.3 (same software/version)
  - Exact match on `/Producer`: +0.1
  - Matching vendor name in submission fields: +0.1
- **Threshold:** If the similarity score is ≥ 0.5, flag as `DUPLICATE_TEMPLATE_MATCH` and route to manual review
- **If the similarity score is ≥ 0.8** (template + creator + vendor match): flag as `DUPLICATE_HIGH_CONFIDENCE`

### Layer 3: Cross-Client Scope

- Both Layer 1 and Layer 2 must function across the **entire** receipt corpus, not limited to specific clients
- The hash index and metadata fingerprint storage must be global
- This is crucial — the exact situation we identified involved receipts from three distinct client companies

### Dashboard Integration

- A new column in the admin review queue: `Duplicate Status` (`None`, `Exact Match`, `Template Match`, `High Confidence`)
- Clicking the flag will display the matched records side-by-side
- Manual override option available: reviewers can mark a flagged submission as `Verified Legitimate` with a note

### Policy Integration (per Elena's directive)

- Following the CEO's directive (2026-03-15): all stipend submissions exceeding $100 require mandatory manual verification regardless of duplicate status
- The `$100_THRESHOLD_MANUAL_REVIEW` rule should be incorporated into the `DecisionEngine` as a policy exception — even if CategoryMatcher, PolicyValidator, AND DuplicateDetector all approve, submissions above $100 should be directed to the manual queue
- This serves as a separate control from duplicate detection but should be included in the same release

---

## Implementation Notes

- Lena confirmed in #engineering that file hashes are already computed at S3 upload — Layer 1 essentially integrates existing data into the validation pipeline
- Layer 2 necessitates a lightweight PDF metadata parser. Python `PyPDF2` or `pikepdf` can extract `/Creator`, `/Producer`, and custom fields. Minimal new dependency required.
- Fingerprint storage: a simple PostgreSQL table with indexed hash and metadata columns. No need for a vector database at this scale.
- Estimated effort: 2-3 days for Layer 1 + Layer 2, plus 1 day for dashboard integration, plus 1 day for implementing the $100 threshold rule

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] SHA-256 hash calculated and stored for each receipt at ingestion
- [ ] Exact hash match prevents auto-approval and directs to manual review
- [ ] PDF metadata extracted and fingerprinted during ingestion
- [ ] Template similarity scoring flags submissions meeting the threshold
- [ ] Cross-client scope: duplicate checks conducted against the global corpus, not limited to specific clients
- [ ] Dashboard displays duplicate status and side-by-side comparisons
- [ ] $100 threshold manual review rule implemented in the DecisionEngine
- [ ] Backfill: run Layer 1 + Layer 2 against all existing Airtable receipt records and flag any additional matches
- [ ] Unit tests for hash matching, metadata extraction, similarity scoring, and threshold routing

## Related

- ENG-216 — Comprehensive audit of all 47 auto-processed pilot expenses (where this issue was discovered)
- ENG-214 — Vectral configuration fix
- ENG-215 — Mosaic configuration fix
- ENG-212 — Pipeline fortification
- CEO directive (2026-03-15) — $100 manual verification threshold", "is_archived": false, "labels_json": "["expense-tool", "audit", "receipt-fraud", "pilot-incident", "security"]", "completed_at": null, "relations_json": "[]", "time_in_status_minutes": null}

{"id": "issue_pipeline_hardening", "title": "[P1] Harden policy ingestion pipeline — remove silent fallback, add type enforcement", "number": 217, "team_id": "team_engineering", "cycle_id": null, "due_date": "2026-03-20", "estimate": 5, "priority": 1, "state_id": "state_in_progress", "parent_id": null, "created_at": "2026-03-18T16:35:00Z", "creator_id": null, "identifier": null, "links_json": "[]", "project_id": "proj_expense_autocategorizer", "started_at": null, "updated_at": "2026-03-18T16:35:00Z", "archived_at": null, "assignee_id": "moveops_lena_bjorkstrom", "canceled_at": null, "description": "## Summary
Fix the policy ingestion pipeline so the two root causes identified in the pilot incidents can never recur.

## Changes Required

### 1. Remove silent fallback to default templates
The current pipeline catches parse errors on individual spreadsheet rows and falls back to the default template. This caused Vectral's config to have wrong limits.

**Fix:** Parse errors abort the entire import with a clear error message: `PolicyIngestionError: Failed to parse row for client '{client_id}' — {error_detail}. Import aborted. No configs were updated.`

### 2. Strict type enforcement on JSON output
The spreadsheet parser reads cell values as-is, preserving text formatting. This caused Mosaic's `max_amount` to be strings and `excluded_categories` to be a string.

**Fix:** Add a type coercion + validation step after parsing:
- `max_amount` → `Number()` with NaN check
- `excluded_categories` → must be Array, reject if string/number/null
- `requires_receipt` → must be Boolean
- `client_id` → must be non-empty String

### 3. Schema validation step before config deployment
Add a validation gate between config generation and deployment:
- JSON Schema validation against the policy config schema
- Diff against previous config version — flag unexpected changes
- Log all changes for audit trail
- Validation failure blocks deployment

### 4. PolicyValidator runtime type guards
Add defensive type checks in PolicyValidator itself:
- `typeof max_amount !== 'number'` → throw `ConfigValidationError`
- `!Array.isArray(excluded_categories)` → throw `ConfigValidationError`
- `Number()` coercion with NaN check as last-resort defense

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Parse error on any row aborts import with clear error
- [ ] All output fields have correct types enforced
- [ ] Schema validation gate blocks malformed configs from deploying
- [ ] PolicyValidator throws on unexpected types instead of silently proceeding
- [ ] Config diff logged on every update
- [ ] All existing tests still pass

## Due: Thursday 3/20

## Related
- ENG-209 (original ingestion pipeline)
- ENG-214 (Vectral config fix — immediate correction deployed)

{"id": "issue_dashboard_type_validation", "title": "Dashboard: Add type validation to policy config editor", "number": 218, "team_id": "team_engineering", "cycle_id": null, "due_date": "2026-03-21", "estimate": 2, "priority": 2, "state_id": "state_in_progress", "parent_id": null, "created_at": "2026-03-18T16:40:00Z", "creator_id": null, "identifier": null, "links_json": "[]", "project_id": "proj_expense_autocategorizer", "started_at": null, "updated_at": "2026-03-18T16:40:00Z", "archived_at": null, "assignee_id": "moveops_anh_nguyen", "canceled_at": null, "description": "Add frontend validation to the admin dashboard's policy config editor to prevent malformed configs from being saved.

## Validation Rules
- `max_amount` fields must be numeric — reject form submission with error message if not
- `excluded_categories` must serialize as an array — handle the single-item multi-select edge case where JS might output a string
- Required fields: `client_id` (non-empty), at least one category with a positive limit
- Display clear inline validation errors next to the offending fields

## Context
This is the third layer of defense alongside Lena's pipeline type enforcement (ENG-217) and Dmitri's schema validation tests (ENG-212). Even if one layer fails, the others should catch malformed data.

## Due: End of this week (3/21)

Parent epic: ENG-203 (Admin Dashboard)", "is_archived": false, "labels_json": "["expense-tool", "frontend", "validation"]", "completed_at": null, "relations_json": "[]", "time_in_status_minutes": null}

{"id": "issue_launch_readiness_checklist", "title": "Define launch readiness checklist for features with automated financial decisions", "number": 219, "team_id": "team_engineering", "cycle_id": null, "due_date": "2026-03-21", "estimate": 2, "priority": 2, "state_id": "state_todo", "parent_id": null, "created_at": "2026-03-18T16:45:00Z", "creator_id": null, "identifier": null, "links_json": "[]", "project_id": "proj_expense_autocategorizer", "started_at": null, "updated_at": "2026-03-18T16:45:00Z", "archived_at": null, "assignee_id": "moveops_samira_tariq", "canceled_at": null, "description": "## Context
Per Elena's directive and the pilot incident post-mortem: we launched the expense auto-categorizer before validation tests were complete. The testing gaps were flagged before launch but we proceeded under time pressure. This resulted in 4 false rejections and 3 false approvals across both pilot clients, with $1,550+ in financial exposure.

## Requirements
Define a launch readiness checklist that must be satisfied before any feature that makes automated financial decisions can go to production.

Proposed checklist:
- [ ] All validation tests passing (unit, integration, schema validation)
- [ ] Config data verified against authoritative source (ops spreadsheet, client contracts)
- [ ] Edge case test coverage reviewed and signed off by at least 2 engineers
- [ ] AM sign-off that they've reviewed the tool's outputs in staging with real data
- [ ] Ops sign-off on category definitions and policy configs
- [ ] Finance review of financial decision logic (approve/deny thresholds)
- [ ] 48-hour shadow mode (system makes decisions but doesn't execute them; humans review for accuracy)
- [ ] Rollback plan documented

## Deliverable
Include in the Friday 3/21 status update to Elena. Formalize as a team standard.

## Due: Friday 3/21", "is_archived": false, "labels_json": "["expense-tool", "process", "pilot-incident"]", "completed_at": null, "relations_json": "[]", "time_in_status_minutes": null}

{"id": "issue_heartland_q1_recon", "title": "Vendor invoice reconciliation — Heartland Movers Q1 2026 (HM-2026-Q1-0042)", "number": 342, "team_id": "team_relocations", "cycle_id": null, "due_date": "2026-04-10", "estimate": 3, "priority": 2, "state_id": "state_in_progress", "parent_id": null, "created_at": "2026-03-25T22:30:00Z", "creator_id": null, "identifier": null, "links_json": "[]", "project_id": null, "started_at": null, "updated_at": "2026-03-25T22:30:00Z", "archived_at": null, "assignee_id": "moveops_chloe_vance", "canceled_at": null, "description": "## Summary

Heartland Movers submitted their Q1 2026 invoice (HM-2026-Q1-0042) billing for 8 moves at $1,600 each ($12,800 total). Cross-referencing against our Airtable Relocations table (tblRelocations01) revealed only **5 of 8 moves are legitimate Heartland charges**, resulting in a **$4,800 overbilling**.

## Disputed Line Items

### 1. Lily Marchetti — Nashville → Chicago (Canopy Health) — $1,600
- **Reason:** Move was CANCELLED. Employee decided not to relocate.
- **Evidence:** Cancellation email sent to Jake Loomis (Heartland) on **Feb 10, 2026**. No acknowledgment received.
- **Airtable:** RELOC-2026-009 — Status: Cancelled

### 2. Danielle Osei — Phoenix → Denver (Vectral Systems) — $1,600
- **Reason:** Move was CANCELLED. Client pulled relocation request (budget reallocation).
- **Evidence:** Cancellation email sent to Jake Loomis on **Feb 13, 2026**. No acknowledgment received.
- **Airtable:** RELOC-2026-010 — Status: Cancelled

### 3. Priya Venkatesh — Houston → Seattle (GreenStack Energy) — $1,600
- **Reason:** Move was completed by **Swift Relocations**, not Heartland. Electronics-specialist crew was required.
- **Airtable:** RELOC-2026-015 — Vendor: Swift Relocations

## Actions Taken
- Payment hold placed on the full $12,800 invoice in QuickBooks (Marcus Thorne)
- Formal dispute email sent to Jake Loomis at Heartland (Marcus Thorne, Mar 25)
- Discrepancy flagged in vendor reconciliation tracker VRECON-2026-Q1-HM (Hana Kim)
- Cancellation emails + Airtable export attached to dispute email as evidence

## Checklist

- [x] Verify all 8 line items against Airtable Relocations table
- [x] Attach cancellation email evidence for Marchetti (Feb 10) and Osei (Feb 13)
- [x] Confirm Swift Relocations handled the Venkatesh move (not Heartland)
- [ ] Track Heartland's response to the formal dispute
- [ ] Receive corrected invoice for $8,000 (5 verified moves)
- [ ] Release payment hold in QuickBooks once corrected invoice is received
- [ ] Close out reconciliation tracker entry VRECON-2026-Q1-HM

## References
- QuickBooks bill: HM-2026-Q1-0042 (flagged DISPUTED)
- Vendor reconciliation: VRECON-2026-Q1-HM
- Airtable records: RELOC-2026-003, 005, 008, 009, 010, 011, 015, 016
- #operations thread with full findings breakdown
- #finance summary from Marcus", "is_archived": false, "labels_json": "["vendor-dispute", "reconciliation", "heartland-movers", "finance"]", "completed_at": null, "relations_json": "[]", "time_in_status_minutes": null}
